title: Un-mixing two overlapping dyes
author: OpenDiscover
blurb: Two coloured compounds in the same cuvette, their absorption bands overlapping across the visible spectrum. By Beer's law the measured absorbance is just their sum — but the inverse question is the useful one: how much of each is there? Fit the mixture spectrum to the two known pure-component shapes by least squares and the concentrations fall right out (here 20 and 15). This spectral un-mixing is how a plate reader tells apart co-eluting metabolites, how pulse oximeters read oxy- vs deoxy-haemoglobin, and how any multi-analyte colorimetric assay is quantified — as long as the two spectra are different enough to be separable.
